title: ALERT Act
plain_summary: The ALERT Act makes new rules for how government agencies create regulations. Agencies must regularly send detailed information about their new regulations to a special government office. That office must then put all this information on the internet for everyone to see. New regulations cannot start working until people have been able to see the information about them for six months, except in emergencies.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for considerati

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the All Economic Regulations are Transparent Act or the ALERT Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs Publication of Information Relating to Rules
sections.text: This section adds new rules to federal law about how government agencies must share information about regulations they create. Every month, the head of each government agency must send specific information to the Administrator of the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs. This office is part of the government that reviews regulations. For each rule the agency expects to propose or finalize in the next twelve months, agencies must provide a summary of what the rule is about, including its identification numbers and file numbers. They must explain why they need the rule and what law gives them the power to make it, including any court or legal deadlines they must meet. They must say whether the law stops them from studying the costs and benefits of the rule, and if not, whether they plan to study these effects. They must say if they plan to skip the normal public comment process that is usually required. They must explain what stage the rulemaking process is in when they submit their report. They must say if the rule must be reviewed every ten years under existing law. For rules they expect to finalize in the next twelve months that have already been proposed to the public, agencies must provide an approximate schedule for finishing the rule. They must estimate whether the rule will cost less than fifty million dollars, between fifty million and one hundred million dollars, between one hundred million and five hundred million dollars, between five hundred million and one billion dollars, between one billion and five billion dollars, between five billion and ten billion dollars, or ten billion dollars or more. They must provide any estimates they considered about the economic effects of the rule, including costs the government puts on state and local governments without giving them money to pay for these costs, and any estimates about how the rule will affect the number of jobs in the United States. If they have no such estimates, they must say so clearly. They must list all important scientific information they used or plan to use related to the rule, including any plans for having other scientists review this information. For each piece of scientific information, they must provide the date they received or expect to receive the information, the date it was or will be made public, and if that date changes in later reports they must briefly explain why, and the internet address where people can find this information once it is completed and made public. The Administrator must make all this information available to the public on the internet within thirty days of receiving it from agencies. Every year by October first, the Administrator must publish in the Federal Register a report about the previous year that includes all the information received from agency heads, the number and list of rules proposed by each agency including whether the agency studied the costs and benefits, the number and list of rules finalized by each agency including whether the agency studied costs and benefits, whether the agency skipped normal public comment procedures, and whether the rule was required by law or left to agency choice. The report must also include the number and list of agency actions that repealed a rule, reduced the scope of a rule, reduced the cost of a rule, or made a rule expire sooner. It must include the total cost of all proposed or finalized rules without subtracting any benefits, the total cost of unfunded mandates imposed by these rules, and the number of rules for which no cost estimate was available. The Administrator must also make available on the internet by October first each year the cost and benefit analysis for each proposed or final rule if one was conducted, the file numbers and identification numbers for each proposed or final rule, the number and list of rules reviewed by the Director of the Office of Management and Budget and the legal authority for each review, the number and list of rules for which agencies completed their required ten year reviews, the number and list of rules submitted to the Comptroller General as required by existing law, and the number and list of rules for which Congress introduced resolutions of disapproval. A new rule cannot take effect until the required information about it has been available on the internet for at least six months. This waiting period does not apply to rules where the agency claims an emergency exception or rules that the President determines by executive order should take effect immediately because they are necessary due to an imminent threat to health or safety or other emergency, necessary to enforce criminal laws, necessary for national security, or issued to implement an international trade agreement. This chapter defines agency, agency action, rule, and rulemaking using the same definitions found elsewhere in federal law, and defines unfunded mandate using the definition from the Congressional Budget Act of nineteen seventy four. The law also updates the table of contents in federal law to include this new chapter. Agencies must start making their first monthly submissions within thirty days of when this law is enacted, and then monthly after that. The yearly reporting requirements begin sixty days after the law is enacted, with the first October first deadline being the first one that occurs after the requirements take effect. For the first yearly publication, agencies must include cost and benefit analyses from the previous ten years. The six month waiting period for new rules to take effect begins eight months after this law is enacted.
